buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Current S&P 500 constituents (as of today)
current_sp500 = pd.read_csv('sp500_current.csv')['ticker'].tolist()

# Backtest from 2010 using CURRENT constituents
for ticker in current_sp500:
    prices = pd.read_csv(f'data/{ticker}.csv', parse_dates=['date'], index_col='date')
    # Only stocks currently IN the index are tested
    # Stocks that were in the index in 2010 but later removed are excluded
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

# Point-in-time S&P 500 constituents — who was in the index on each date
constituents = pd.read_csv('sp500_historical_constituents.csv', parse_dates=['date'])

def get_sp500_on_date(as_of_date):
    """Returns stocks that were in the S&P 500 on a specific date."""
    snapshot = constituents[
        (constituents['added_date'] <= as_of_date) &
        ((constituents['removed_date'].isna()) | (constituents['removed_date'] > as_of_date))
    ]
    return snapshot['ticker'].tolist()

# Backtest uses different universe for each date
for date in backtest_dates:
    universe = get_sp500_on_date(date)
    # Includes stocks that were later removed (restructured, delisted, etc.)
description: Backtest uses current index constituents instead of historical point-in-time membership.
explanation: The S&P 500 composition changes constantly. Companies get added after performing well and removed after performing poorly or going bankrupt. Backtesting with current constituents means you only test on stocks that survived — you never test on stocks that were removed due to poor performance. This is survivorship bias at the index level, which can add 1-3% per year of phantom alpha.
suggested_fix: Use a historical point-in-time constituent database that tracks additions and removals.

issues.title: Backtest uses current index constituents causing survivorship bias
